topic: The Early Church: The Church in Acts
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What is distinctive about the ontological argument?
items.correct: It tries to prove God's existence purely from the concept of God — a priori reasoning needing no empirical evidence about the world.
items.misconception: The ontological argument uses scientific evidence to prove God exists.
items.random_wrong: The ontological argument is the same as the cosmological argument.
items.why: Anselm's argument is famously a priori — pure reasoning from definition. Students try to fit it into evidence-based frameworks, missing what makes it uniquely controversial in philosophy.

items.q: How do samsara, karma and moksha fit together in Hindu thought?
items.correct: Samsara is the cycle of rebirth; karma is the law of action and consequence that drives rebirth; moksha is liberation from samsara — these form a connected chain, not three independent ideas.
items.misconception: Samsara, karma and moksha are three separate Hindu deities.
items.random_wrong: Moksha is the Hindu word for heaven.
items.why: Students learn the terms as a vocabulary list and miss the structural relationship: karma keeps you in samsara, and moksha releases you. Without the connection, the soteriology doesn't make sense.

items.q: What do Christians mean by resurrection, and how does it differ from reincarnation?
items.correct: Resurrection is the once-for-all bodily raising to eternal life at the end of time (modelled on Christ's resurrection); reincarnation is the soul's repeated rebirth into new bodies. They are distinct doctrines from different religious traditions.
items.misconception: Resurrection and reincarnation are basically the same idea of life after death.
items.random_wrong: Christians believe everyone is reincarnated until they accept Jesus.
items.why: Christianity affirms one bodily resurrection, not cyclical rebirth. Students flatten different afterlife concepts into a single 'life after death' idea, missing the very different metaphysics involved.
Correct answer: Aquinas. “Anselm” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Hume. “Descartes” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many of Aristotle's causes are there?
items.why: The real figure is 4 causes. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
